1.description: The market for mortgage intermediation. The vertical axis shows the price of intermediation,ϕ$\phi$. The supply curve for intermediation is assumed to be upward sloping, and the demand curve downward sloping. Changes in MBS prices,pTBA$p_{TBA}$, shift the demand curve and affect the equilibrium price and quantity. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

2.description: An example of a rate sheet.

3.description: Mortgage rates over our sample period. The line in Panel A shows the median rate across lenders at which a rebate of +1 point is offered (“Rate101”). The shaded area is the 10thto 90thpercentile range of Rate101 offers across lenders in our data. Panel B shows the difference between Rate101 and Rate100 (for a rebate of zero), and between Rate101 and the Freddie Mac Primary Mortgage Market Survey series for 30-year fixed-rate mortgages. All series are weekly averages. For the Rate101 − Freddie Mac series, the Rate101 series is restricted to the days that are reported in the weekly Freddie Mac series. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

4.description: Event studies of major monetary policy (QE) announcements over our sample period. The top lines show MBS prices (pTBA$p_{TBA}$) and median rebates offered to borrowers (pYSP$p_{YSP}$), holding the rate fixed at Rate101 (Rate100 in 2008 to 2009) of the day prior to the announcement. The middle line shows the pass-through of the TBA price change to YSPs. The bottom line shows daily application counts as measured by HMDA (nonweekends/nonholidays only). In each of the panels, the price data shown are based only on lenders who are present in the data for the entire event window. Note that in the top left panel, November 27 was Thanksgiving, which explains the missing data point and the low applications on Friday, November 28. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

5.description: High-frequency pass-through. The figure provides a scatterplot of daily first differences of rebates (YSPs) and MBS prices. Gray dots show every data point (for the full sample period October 2008 to October 2014), while black dots show averages by deciles ofΔpTBA$\Delta p_{TBA}$(“binscatter”). The diagonal line is the 45∘$^\circ$line, where points would lie with perfect pass-through. Both the YSP and MBS series correspond to Rate101, as discussed in SectionIV, and reflect medians across lenders. These data underlie the regressions in TableIII. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

6.description: Evolution of price of intermediation over time. In Panel A,ϕ$\phi$denotes the monthly estimated average price of intermediation (calculated as the difference between the MBS market value of a loan with note rate Rate101 and the rebate offered to a borrower) across lenders in our sample, andπ$\pi$is the corresponding average gain-on-sale (which adjusts for the net cost of servicing). The dashed line shows the estimated linear time trend in theϕ$\phi$series. Panel B shows the monthly averageϕ$\phi$after detrending (and shifting the series so as to keep the average level the same as in Panel A), plotted against mortgage applications per business day (from HMDA). The correlation between detrendedϕ$\phi$andApplicationsin Panel B is 0.78. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

7.description: Gap between the weighted-average coupon and 10-year Treasury yield, and relation to applications. Panel A shows the weighted-average coupon (WAC) on outstanding conventional MBS (from J.P. Morgan Markets) and the 10-year constant-maturity Treasury yield (from FRED) over our sample. Panel B shows the difference between the WAC and 10-year yield, which is a proxy for the average refinancing incentive of outstanding mortgages, against the monthly number of new loan applications (divided by the number of business days), from HMDA. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

8.description: Loan application volume and median processing time. This figure shows the evolution of the total number of new loan applications in a month (divided by the number of business days in that month) and the median processing time for loan applications submitted in the corresponding month, both from HMDA data. Processing time is calculated as the number of days between the application date and the “action” date, when either the loan closes or the application is denied or withdrawn. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

9.description: Estimated differences in the price of intermediation across MSAs. The figure shows estimated MSA fixed effects (with Los Angeles as the omitted base category) and corresponding 95% confidence intervals based on regressions of the monthly price of intermediation on lender-by-month fixed effects and MSA fixed effects. The price of intermediation is calculated in percent of the loan amount and under the assumption thatpTBA$p_{TBA}$is identical across MSAs. Panel A shows estimates for the entire 2009 to 2014 period; Panel B for 2009 to 2010 only, and Panel C for 2013 to 2014 only. Standard errors underlying confidence intervals are clustered by MSA and lender.

10.description: Measuring the profits from intermediation. This figure shows time series of the estimated price of intermediation and its components. As explained in the text,ϕ$\phi$is our broadest measure of the price of intermediation.π$\pi$(gain-on-sale) nets out the direct costs of servicing the loan. To constructκ$\kappa$, the direct cost of originating a loan, we use data from the Mortgage Bankers Association on all loan production costs less income received by the intermediary directly from the borrower. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
